definition: A mechanism that allows models to focus on relevant parts of the input when producing output. Computes weighted sums of value vectors based on query-key compatibility.
A neural network architecture based entirely on self-attention mechanisms without recurrence or convolution. Introduced in the 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
A dense vector representation of text that captures semantic meaning. Similar texts have similar embeddings in the vector space.
A metric measuring the cosine of the angle between two vectors. Ranges from -1 to 1 where 1 means identical direction.
A specialized database optimized for storing, indexing, and querying high-dimensional vectors. Examples include ChromaDB, Pinecone, and FAISS.
Hierarchical Navigable Small World. An algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search that builds a multi-layer graph structure for fast retrieval.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation. A technique that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base.
The process of splitting documents into smaller pieces for embedding and retrieval. Chunk size is a critical hyperparameter affecting retrieval quality.
The process of continuing to train a pre-trained model on a specific dataset to adapt it for a particular task or domain.
The practice of crafting input prompts to elicit desired behavior from language models. Includes techniques like few-shot examples and chain-of-thought.
When a language model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect or unsupported information.
A model that takes a pair of texts as input and produces a relevance score. More accurate than bi-encoders but slower, used for re-ranking.
A model that independently encodes two texts into vectors for comparison. Fast for retrieval but less accurate than cross-encoders.
A sparse retrieval algorithm based on term frequency and inverse document frequency. Good for keyword matching, often combined with dense retrieval.
Hypothetical Document Embeddings. A technique that generates a hypothetical answer to a query and uses its embedding for retrieval instead of the query embedding.
Maximal Marginal Relevance. A retrieval strategy that balances relevance and diversity by penalizing chunks similar to already-selected ones.
LangChain Expression Language. A declarative syntax for composing LangChain components using the pipe (|) operator.
The basic unit of text that language models process. Can be a word,  subword or character depending on the tokenizer.
The maximum number of tokens a language model can process in a single input. Determines how much retrieved context can be included.
A parameter controlling the randomness of LLM output. Lower values produce more deterministic responses; higher values increase creativity.
A framework for evaluating RAG pipelines across dimensions like faithfulness and context precision.
